papers.title: P. succinatutens: Succinate Scavenging & Pathogen Inhibition
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens scavenges luminal succinate through its obligate succinate-to-propionate metabolic pathway, depriving opportunistic pathogens (Enterobacteriaceae) of a critical carbon source while simultaneously lowering luminal pH via propionate production. The methylmalonyl-CoA pathway is fully validated in vitro and in murine mono-colonization models (Level 3). Core enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) show 100% amino acid sequence identity between two Phascolarctobacterium type strains. In porcine models, this function reaches only Level 1 (co-occurrence network inference). Critical evidence gap (Level 4 void): porcine-derived strain isolation and mono-colonization in weaned piglets is entirely absent.
papers.modelSystem: In vitro + Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine co-occurrence network (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived strain isolated; no mono-colonization in piglets performed. Translational bottleneck: murine L3 to porcine L4.
papers.forwardJustification: P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in vitro. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 100% conserved. Murine mono-colonization validates causal propionate production.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host control of P. succinatutens abundance or activity. Reverse pathway entirely uncharacterized in any model species.
papers.summary: Benchmark case for the translational gap. P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway is mechanistically validated at L3 in mice but only inferred from co-occurrence networks at L1 in pigs. Represents the critical evidence void at the murine-to-porcine translational interface. B12-dependence of the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway creates a functional intersection between the probiotic and Vitamin B12 nodes.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Intestinal Barrier Maintenance via Propionate
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens maintains intestinal barrier integrity through propionate via a dual-pathway mechanism: (1) membrane pathway: GPR43/GPR41 activation rerouting signals through beta-arrestin and cAMP branches to block NF-kappaB nuclear translocation; (2) intracellular pathway: HDAC inhibition relieving epigenetic repression of tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin). In murine models, the complete causal chain of mono-colonization to propionate production to TLR4/NF-kappaB inhibition to barrier maintenance is established (Level 3). Two Level 2 studies in pigs independently validated propionate-mediated TLR4/NF-kappaB suppression and tight junction protein upregulation. Key limitation: porcine evidence validates propionate's barrier-protective effect but does not demonstrate that P. succinatutens produces sufficient propionate in situ for this effect.
papers.modelSystem: Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine dietary fiber fermentation models (L2)
papers.keyLimitation: Strain-specific causal validation missing in pigs: propionate's barrier effect validated, but P. succinatutens' in situ propionate production capacity in porcine gut not demonstrated.
papers.summary: Best-validated probiotic function node. Dual-pathway propionate signaling mechanism fully characterized. Translational gap: strain-specific propionate production quantification in porcine gut lumen and epithelium needed to advance from L2 to L3 in pigs.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Lipid Deposition Reduction via MYC-Arginine-FOXO3a Axis
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens reduces host lipid deposition through the propionate-mediated MYC-arginine-FOXO3a molecular axis: HDAC inhibition leads to MYC transcriptional attenuation, derepression of glutamine transporters (SLC1A5/SLC38A5) enhances de novo arginine biosynthesis, and arginine accumulation suppresses PI3K/Akt/FOXO3a signaling to reduce adipogenic gene expression. Murine FMT and mono-colonization experiments have fully validated this pathway (Level 3). In porcine models, UCP1 knock-in pigs show negative correlation between fecal SCFAs and adipogenic gene expression (Level 1 association). The pathway is not P. succinatutens-specific in the porcine context. Functional divergence from P. faecium (which operates via TLR2) constitutes genus-level metabolic specialization.
papers.modelSystem: Murine FMT + mono-colonization (L3); Porcine UCP1 knock-in correlation (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: Pathway validated in mice only; porcine evidence is SCFA-adipogenic gene correlation, not P. succinatutens-specific. Pathway conservation across commercial pig breeds is speculative.
papers.summary: Demonstrates genus-level functional divergence (P. succinatutens vs P. faecium). MYC-arginine-FOXO3a axis is a potential target for lipid reduction in livestock. Porcine validation remains entirely at association level.

papers.title: Butyrate/SCFAs: Compartmentalized Co-Metabolism Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Butyrate is the most extensively validated node in the compartmentalized co-metabolism network. Forward pathway (microbial metabolites to host epigenetics): Level 2b in vivo validation in porcine models. Xiong et al. (2016) landmark study demonstrated butyrate upregulates endogenous host defense peptides via HDAC inhibition in weaned piglets. Porcine ileal organoid experiments confirmed HDAC inhibition and barrier-enhancing effects. Tibetan pig studies showed butyrate promotes macrophage H4K31 crotonylation via mTORC1-HDAC3/8. Reverse pathway (host epigenetics to microbiota): only Level 1. Host gene regulatory networks (PRDM15, STAT1) and germ-free pig transcriptome data provide hypothesis-generating clues. Coupling: Level 0. Forward and reverse effects never simultaneously observed in a single experimental system. Measurement Depth: Level 0. All studies measured only a single compartment (luminal) at a single timepoint. The three-order-of-magnitude concentration difference between luminal (mM) and intracellular (muM) butyrate has not been incorporated into experimental design.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, Tibetan pigs) + ileal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Forward pathway at L2b but reverse pathway at L1 and coupling at L0. The fundamental butyrate concentration gradient (lumen mM vs epithelium muM) has never been incorporated into experimental design. No study has simultaneously measured forward and reverse effects.
papers.summary: Most-validated co-metabolism node. Forward pathway well-characterized at L2b in pigs (HDAC inhibition, host defense peptides, crotonylation). But reverse pathway, coupling verification, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0-L1. Represents the core evidence asymmetry in the co-metabolism framework.

papers.title: Bile Acids: Host-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Bile acids are a porcine-validated core node. Forward pathway: Level 2b. Hyodeoxycholic acid (HDCA), the dominant porcine bile acid, enhances intestinal barrier function via FXR-PI3K/AKT pathway in weaned piglets, increasing Lactobacillus abundance from 5.28% to 37.97%. Neonatal piglets show bile acid-mediated epithelial proliferation and barrier maturation through TGR5 activation and FXR downregulation. Substantial species differences in bile acid pool composition (pigs: HDCA-dominant; mice: CA/DCA-dominant) limit murine evidence extrapolation. Reverse pathway: only Level 1. Whether host bile acid signaling alterations feed back to regulate microbial community composition has not been causally validated. Coupling: Level 0. FXR/TGR5 activation and microbial restructuring never simultaneously tracked. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Main evidence gap: concentration gradient data for bile acids at the lumen-epithelium interface are absent.
papers.keyLimitation: Species-specific bile acid composition (HDCA-dominant in pigs) renders murine data non-directly translatable. Reverse pathway causal validation void is the critical bottleneck for understanding bile acid-microbiota feedback circuits.
papers.summary: Porcine-validated forward pathway with species-specific bile acid (HDCA). Lactobacillus expansion from 5.3% to 38.0% is the strongest microbial community shift documented in any node. Reverse pathway (host-to-microbiome bile acid signaling) is the critical evidence gap. FXR-dependent antimicrobial peptide pathway recently identified as potential reverse mechanism.

papers.title: Tryptophan Metabolites: AhR Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Tryptophan metabolites are partially porcine-validated. Forward pathway: Level 2a (preliminary in vivo). Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron alleviates weaning diarrhea in piglets via tryptophan metabolism-AhR-Nrf2 signaling. Gut microbiota-mediated tryptophan metabolism suppresses pyroptosis via AhR signaling to maintain intestinal epithelial homeostasis in piglets. Dietary tryptophan supplementation modulates gut microbial composition and activates AhR signaling. Validations are based on specific bacterial strains or dietary interventions, not direct causal validation of tryptophan metabolites per se. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Tryptophan metabolite reverse regulatory role completely unexplored. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gap: concentration gradients of tryptophan metabolites (indole, kynurenine) across lumen-epithelium-microenvironment compartments never simultaneously measured. Temporal asynchrony between AhR activation and microbial community shifts uncharacterized.
papers.keyLimitation: Validations are strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) or dietary, not direct metabolite causal validation. AhR activation and pyroptosis suppression not linked causally in porcine gut in vivo. Reverse pathway entirely unexplored.
papers.summary: AhR signaling is the central mechanism linking tryptophan metabolites to intestinal homeostasis. Strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) and dietary intervention evidence at L2a. Direct metabolite causal validation, reverse pathway, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0.

papers.title: Polyamines: Cross-Species Extrapolation Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Polyamines are a cross-species extrapolated predicted node with no direct validation evidence at any level in porcine models. Forward pathway: only Level 1. All existing evidence derives from murine models and in vitro studies: polyamines regulate histone methylation and acetylation by promoting nucleosome condensation; polyamines and gut microbiota form bidirectional interactions maintaining intestinal homeostasis (including microbial growth and biofilm formation). None of these mechanisms have been experimentally verified in the porcine gut. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: baseline concentration and spatial distribution data for polyamines in the porcine gut are entirely absent; polyamine biosynthetic capacity of porcine-derived strains uncharacterized; polyamine transporter expression patterns in porcine intestinal epithelium unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine models and in vitro only
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine data at any level. Baseline polyamine concentrations in porcine gut unknown. Porcine-derived polyamine-producing strains not isolated. Entirely dependent on cross-species extrapolation from murine evidence.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence base among all five nodes in porcine context. All evidence is murine-derived. Inclusion based on established mammalian host-microbe co-metabolic functions. Porcine functional relevance requires de novo establishment. First essential step: baseline polyamine concentration measurement in porcine gut compartments.

papers.title: Vitamin B12: One-Carbon Metabolism Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Vitamin B12 has the weakest evidence base among all five nodes. Forward pathway: only Level 1 (association inference). B12 is a key cofactor in host one-carbon metabolism, participating in S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) synthesis to provide methyl donors for DNA methylation. This theoretical link is elaborated in reviews but direct causal validation between B12 concentrations and DNA methylation marks is absent in porcine models. In P. succinatutens' succinate-to-propionate pathway, B12 is the essential coenzyme for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. B12 availability directly impacts propionate production efficiency, representing the functional intersection with Project 1. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: B12 concentration distribution across porcine gut compartments unknown; B12-propionate metabolic coupling never validated in situ in porcine gut; causal effect of B12 on porcine intestinal epithelial DNA methylation untested.
papers.modelSystem: Theoretical/review-level only; no direct experimental validation
papers.keyLimitation: Weakest of all five nodes. Entirely at theoretical/review level. No experimental data on B12-DNA methylation link in porcine gut. B12-propionate coupling in situ completely untested. P. succinatutens B12 dependence is the only functional anchor in the co-metabolism network.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence node but critical for understanding one-carbon metabolism integration with microbial SCFA production. Functional intersection with P. succinatutens (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase requires B12) provides the mechanistic rationale for inclusion. Entirely a hypothesis awaiting experimental testing in porcine models.

papers.title: Propionate: Succinate-to-Propionate Pathway Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Propionate is a major microbial SCFA in the porcine gut, produced via the succinate pathway (dominant in Bacteroidetes and Phascolarctobacterium) and the acrylate pathway (dominant in Firmicutes). Forward pathway (microbial propionate → host): Level 2b in pigs. Propionate activates GPR41/GPR43 in porcine intestinal epithelium, suppresses HDAC activity, and serves as a gluconeogenic substrate in the porcine liver. The P. succinatutens methylmalonyl-CoA pathway directly couples succinate consumption to propionate production, representing a B12-dependent metabolic intersection. Reverse pathway (host → microbial propionate): Level 1. Dietary fiber composition and host bile acid secretion patterns influence propionate-producing community structure, but direct host genetic control over propionate output is uncharacterized. Key evidence gap: propionate concentration gradients across the porcine gut epithelium have never been systematically mapped. Bidirectional coupling remains at Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary intervention + in vitro bacterial cultures
papers.keyLimitation: Propionate concentration gradients across gut compartments unmapped. P. succinatutens in situ propionate production rate in porcine gut unknown. Reverse pathway entirely at association level. B12-propionate coupling never measured in vivo.
papers.summary: Propionate is the second-most abundant SCFA in the porcine hindgut and a core output of the succinate-to-propionate pathway. Wraps P. succinatutens metabolism (succinate→propionate), B12 cofactor requirement (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase), and host GPR41/43 signaling into a single integrated node. Forward pathway partially validated at L2b in pigs; reverse pathway and coupling entirely unexplored.
papers.forwardJustification: Propionate-GPR41/43 signaling validated in porcine epithelium. HDAC inhibition and gluconeogenic roles characterized. P. succinatutens pathway fully mapped in vitro.
papers.reverseJustification: No causal evidence for host control of propionate-producing community in pigs. Dietary fiber → propionate association established but no host genetic or immune mechanism characterized.

papers.title: Acetate: Cross-Feeding & Systemic Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Acetate is the most abundant SCFA in the porcine gut (typically 50-60% of total SCFAs) and the primary substrate for butyrate-producing bacteria via cross-feeding. Forward pathway (microbial acetate → host): Level 2a in pigs. Acetate crosses the gut epithelium via MCT1/SMCT1 transporters, enters the portal circulation, and reaches peripheral tissues at high concentrations (100-200 muM in human portal blood; porcine data limited). Acetate activates GPR43 on enteroendocrine L-cells, stimulates GLP-1/PYY secretion, and serves as a lipogenic substrate and epigenetic modifier (acetyl-CoA for histone acetylation). Reverse pathway (host → acetate producers): Level 1. Host diet composition and intestinal transit time influence acetogenic community structure, but host-driven feedback mechanisms are unexplored. Key evidence gap: the gut microbial acetate economy in pigs — production rates, cross-feeding fluxes, and epithelial transport kinetics — lacks systematic quantification.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary fiber models (association level); Murine mono-colonization (causal)
papers.keyLimitation: Acetate cross-feeding network structure in porcine gut unknown. Portal acetate concentration data absent for pigs. Acetate contribution to host histone acetylation in porcine intestinal epithelium never quantified. Reverse pathway entirely at theoretical level.
papers.summary: Acetate is quantitatively dominant but mechanistically understudied in pigs. Its role as the primary cross-feeding substrate for butyrate producers makes it a critical upstream node in the SCFA network. Porcine-specific portal and peripheral acetate kinetics are a major data gap. Histone acetylation link offers a direct epigenetic readout for microbial acetate activity.
papers.forwardJustification: GPR43 activation and GLP-1/PYY secretion by acetate validated in murine models. Acetate-to-butyrate cross-feeding well-characterized in vitro. Porcine evidence limited to dietary fiber → acetate correlations.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host-directed control of acetogenic community composition. Host diet as driver established; host immune/genetic feedback unexplored.

papers.title: Folate/B9: One-Carbon Metabolism & Methyl Donor Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Folate (vitamin B9) is a key microbial product in the gut that participates in host one-carbon metabolism alongside B12. Gut bacteria (especially Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus species) are net folate producers, contributing 10-30% of host folate requirements. Forward pathway (microbial folate → host): Level 1 in pigs. Microbial folate is absorbed in the colon via reduced folate carrier (RFC) and proton-coupled folate transporter (PCFT), contributing to host methyl donor pools (SAM cycle) and nucleotide synthesis. Bifidobacterium adolescentis folate production has been demonstrated in vitro and in murine models, but porcine in vivo evidence is absent. Reverse pathway (host → microbial folate): Level 1. Host dietary folate intake and the host folate absorption efficiency set the competitive landscape for microbial folate producers vs. auxotrophs. In pigs, dietary folic acid supplementation alters gut microbial composition but the mechanism (direct vs. indirect via host metabolism) is unclear. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Key gap: gut luminal folate concentrations and microbial folate production rates in pigs are completely unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine Bifidobacterium mono-association (L2a); Porcine dietary folic acid (L1 association)
papers.keyLimitation: Porcine gut luminal folate concentrations unknown. Microbial folate production rates in pig gut never measured. Bifidobacterium/Lactobacillus folate contribution to host methyl donor pool unquantified in any large animal model. B12-folate metabolic intersection in porcine gut unexplored.
papers.summary: Folate represents the microbial contribution to host one-carbon metabolism at the methyl donor level. Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus are the primary porcine-relevant folate producers. The B12-folate intersection in one-carbon metabolism is mechanistically established in human/murine systems but untested in pigs. Luminal concentration and production rate data are the prerequisite measurements.
papers.forwardJustification: Microbial folate production demonstrated in Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus in vitro. Colonic folate absorption via RFC/PCFT characterized in rodents. Porcine evidence: dietary folic acid changes gut microbiota composition at association level only.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary folate status influences competitive dynamics between folate producers and auxotrophs. Porcine dietary folic acid → microbiota shift observed but mechanistic pathway (direct microbial vs. host-mediated) not resolved.

papers.title: Vitamin A/Retinoic Acid: Immune-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Vitamin A and its active metabolite retinoic acid (RA) sit at the host-microbiome interface, regulating both host immunity and gut microbial ecology. Forward pathway (microbe-influenced RA → host): Level 2a in pigs. Gut bacteria influence host vitamin A metabolism through: (1) microbial production of retinaldehyde dehydrogenase (RALDH) cofactors that modulate host RA synthesis; (2) microbial SCFAs promoting dendritic cell RALDH expression and RA production. Porcine weaning stress decreases intestinal RALDH activity, correlating with microbial community disruption. Reverse pathway (host RA → microbiome): Level 2b in pigs. Retinoic acid directly regulates the gut microbiome through: (1) RA-induced epithelial IL-22/STAT3 signaling altering AMP secretion profiles; (2) RA-RAR signaling in B cells promoting IgA class switching, which shapes microbial composition; (3) RA modulation of Treg/Th17 balance influencing immune tolerance to commensals. This reverse pathway is better characterized than most nodes because the RA-RAR axis is well-studied. Coupling: Level 0 — RA effects on host immunity and microbial composition never tracked simultaneously in pigs. Measurement Depth: Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine weaning stress models (L2a); Murine RAR knockout + gnotobiotic (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: Simultaneous measurement of RA-dependent host immune activation AND microbial community shifts not performed in pigs. Porcine intestinal RA concentration and RALDH activity spatial mapping absent. The RA-RAR-IgA axis relevance to porcine gut commensal control unquantified.
papers.summary: Vitamin A/retinoic acid is the best-characterized REVERSE pathway node. The RA-RAR-IgA axis provides a mechanistically detailed host-to-microbiome signaling route. The forward pathway (microbes → host RA metabolism) is less characterized, creating an unusual reverse-dominant evidence asymmetry. Key AhR-RA crosstalk at the tryptophan-vitamin A metabolic intersection is unexplored in pigs.
papers.forwardJustification: Porcine weaning stress reduces intestinal RALDH activity (enzyme-level evidence). Microbial SCFA promotion of DC RALDH expression established in mice. Causal microbial → RA → host immune effect not demonstrated in pigs.
papers.reverseJustification: RA-RAR signaling in B cell IgA class switching well-characterized (molecular pathway). RA-IL-22-STAT3-AMP axis established. Porcine dietary vitamin A alters gut microbiota composition. Strongest reverse pathway evidence among all non-Bile Acid nodes at L2b.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Porcine Gut Strain Isolation & Characterization Status
papers.abstract: Phascolarctobacterium succinatutens is an obligate succinate-consuming, propionate-producing bacterium in the family Acidaminococcaceae. It occupies a keystone metabolic niche in the porcine gut: its succinate-to-propionate pathway prevents luminal succinate accumulation (which would otherwise fuel Enterobacteriaceae blooms) while producing propionate for host signaling. Current isolation status: YIT 12066 and YIT 12067 were isolated from human feces (Yakult Central Institute, 2012). NO porcine-derived strain has been isolated to date. 16S rRNA gene surveys place Phascolarctobacterium at 1-5% relative abundance in healthy pig feces across diverse breeds (Yorkshire, Landrace, Duroc, Meishan). Metagenomic binning has recovered near-complete P. succinatutens genomes from pig fecal metagenomes. Core metabolic pathway (methylmalonyl-CoA) shows 95-100% amino acid sequence identity between human and porcine metagenome-assembled genomes. Key bottleneck: without a porcine-derived isolate, mono-colonization experiments in piglets cannot be performed, blocking L3→L4 translational advance.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine 16S/metagenomic surveys (L1); Human isolate in vitro (L2); Murine cross-colonization (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived P. succinatutens isolate exists — the single most important bottleneck for advancing the translational pipeline from murine L3 to porcine L4. Without a pig-derived strain, causal validation in piglets is impossible.
papers.summary: P. succinatutens is the highest-priority strain for porcine isolation. Its succinate-to-propionate pathway is the mechanistic center of the co-metabolism framework. The human isolate (YIT 12066/12067) provides a genetic template, but porcine strain isolation is the prerequisite for advancing the entire Project 1 translational pipeline from L3 to L4. Core metabolic pathway is conserved but host-specific colonization factors remain unknown.
papers.forwardJustification: Succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in human isolates. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 95-100% conserved in porcine MAGs. In vitro propionate production demonstrated. Host signaling effects inferred from propionate node.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary and immunological factors influencing P. succinatutens abundance in pig gut completely uncharacterized. Porcine intestinal factors driving niche specialization unknown. Dietary succinate availability is the only characterized host input.

papers.title: Lactobacillus: Porcine Gut Probiotic Ecology Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Lactobacillus species (including Lactiplantibacillus, Limosilactobacillus, and Ligilactobacillus per reclassified taxonomy) are dominant small intestinal colonizers in pigs, reaching 10^7-10^9 CFU/g in the duodenum and jejunum. They are the most extensively studied porcine gut bacteria, with >200 published porcine feeding trials. Forward pathway (Lactobacillus → host): Level 2b in pigs. Multiple porcine trials demonstrate Lactobacillus-mediated pathogen inhibition (E. coli K88, Salmonella Typhimurium), intestinal barrier enhancement (ZO-1, occludin upregulation), and immune modulation (TLR2/TLR4 signaling, IgA promotion). However, strain-level specificity is high — effects observed with one strain (e.g., L. plantarum JDFM LP11) do not generalize. Reverse pathway (host → Lactobacillus): Level 2a in pigs. Host dietary fiber composition, weaning stress, and intestinal mucin glycosylation patterns all influence Lactobacillus community composition. Host antimicrobial peptide secretion (RegIII-gamma) selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in a porcine weaning model. Key gap: strain-level resolution is required — genus-level 16S data obscures functional divergence between species/strains.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, >200 trials) + porcine intestinal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Genus-level data dominate; strain-level functional divergence is critical but under-characterized. Reclassified taxonomy (Lactobacillus → 25 genera, 2020) not reflected in most porcine literature. Strain-specific colonization factors and persistence in porcine gut poorly understood.
papers.summary: Lactobacillus is the most extensively studied porcine gut bacterium but suffers from nomenclatural fragmentation and inadequate strain-level resolution. Forward pathway evidence at L2b (barrier, immune, pathogen inhibition), reverse pathway at L2a (host AMPs and weaning stress). Represents the best-characterized bidirectional system among all strain nodes but lacks coupling (never measured simultaneously in same animals).
papers.forwardJustification: Extensive porcine trial literature: barrier enhancement (ZO-1/occludin), pathogen inhibition (E. coli, Salmonella), immune modulation (TLR2/4, IgA). Strain specificity high — effects not generalizable across Lactobacillus species.
papers.reverseJustification: Porcine weaning stress alters Lactobacillus community structure. Host RegIII-gamma AMP selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in piglets. Dietary fiber → Lactobacillus bloom established. Host mucin glycosylation influence demonstrated at L2a.

papers.title: Prevotella: Porcine Hindgut Dominance & Succinate Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Prevotella is the dominant Gram-negative genus in the porcine hindgut, often representing 15-30% of the fecal metagenome — substantially higher than in humans. Prevotella species are major succinate producers in the pig colon, making them the primary upstream metabolic partner for succinate-consuming bacteria (P. succinatutens). Forward pathway (Prevotella → host): Level 2a in pigs. Prevotella-dominated enterotypes are associated with higher luminal SCFA concentrations and improved feed efficiency in pigs. Prevotella copri produces succinate as a fermentation end-product, which is then cross-fed to propionate producers. Specific Prevotella species encode carbohydrate-active enzymes (CAZymes) targeting porcine dietary fibers (arabinoxylan, pectin). Reverse pathway (host → Prevotella): Level 2a. Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine host genotype (MUC4, FUT1 polymorphisms) influences Prevotella colonization. Key gap: Prevotella-to-P. succinatutens succinate cross-feeding has never been directly measured in the porcine gut in situ. The succinate intermediate pool size and flux rate are unknown.
papers.keyLimitation: Prevotella-to-Phascolarctobacterium succinate cross-feeding never measured in situ in porcine gut. Succinate intermediate pool size unknown. Prevotella species-level functional diversity in pigs poorly characterized — genus-level data mask substantial metabolic heterogeneity.
papers.summary: Prevotella is the dominant succinate producer and primary metabolic partner for P. succinatutens in the porcine hindgut. The Prevotella→succinate→Phascolarctobacterium→propionate cascade is the central metabolite flow in the co-metabolism framework. Host dietary fiber composition is the dominant top-down driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine-specific Prevotella CAZyme repertoire and succinate production kinetics are key unknowns.
papers.forwardJustification: Prevotella-dominated enterotype → higher SCFA and improved feed efficiency in multiple porcine cohorts. P. copri succinate production characterized in vitro. Porcine Prevotella CAZyme diversity documented at metagenomic level. Direct succinate flux to propionate producers not measured.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver — well-established in multiple porcine dietary intervention trials. Host genotype (MUC4, FUT1) influence documented. Weaning transition shifts Prevotella abundance, driven by dietary change.

papers.title: Item recognition is associated with gut microbiota composition in healthy humans.
papers.abstract: Murine studies show that the gut microbiota-the collection of the microbes residing in the large intestine-affects memory performance in the host. However, whether commensal gut bacteria are linked to human episodic memory remains unknown. Here, we investigated whether individual differences in episodic memory performance were associated with differences in the indigenous gut microbiota composition between individuals. papers.title: Probiotic Potential of Indigenous Cetobacterium somerae R9 in Mud Crab (Scylla paramamosain).
papers.abstract: Probiotics provide an efficient and relatively safe method for preventing disease and increasing production in aquaculture. During the screening of beneficial bacteria in the economically important mud crab (Scylla paramamosain), an indigenous gut strain, Cetobacterium somerae R9, was isolated for the first time as a butyrate producer. This study aimed to evaluate the probiotic potential of C. somerae R9 both in vitro and in vivo. In vitro assays revealed that C. somerae R9 exhibited grow at pH

papers.title: Nanocellulose Alleviates Intrahepatic Cholestasis of Pregnancy via Gut Microbiota-Mediated Bile Acid Homeostasis.
papers.abstract: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP) is a pregnancy-specific disorder characterized by elevated maternal serum total bile acid (TBA) and increased risk of adverse fetal outcomes, yet current therapies and preventive strategies remain suboptimal. Here, we present a preclinical investigation of nanocellulose as a gut microbiota-targeted dietary intervention to restore bile acid homeostasis. papers.title: Bile acids in Alzheimer's disease: a double-edged sword in gut-liver-brain signaling and neurodegeneration.
papers.abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by amyloid-β (Aβ) deposition, tau pathology, synaptic dysfunction, neuroinflammation, and metabolic impairment. Increasing evidence suggests that bile acids, traditionally recognized for their roles in lipid digestion and hepatic metabolism, act as endocrine signaling molecules that influence central nervous system (CNS) homeostasis. Harmane induces apoptosis through RRM2B and suppresses colorectal cancer progression.

Harmane, a natural indole alkaloid, is found in Leguminosae plants (particularly those of the Crotalaria genus), as well as in mammalian tissues and certain food products. papers.title: Multi-omics profiling of gut-serum axis dynamics in gestational sows with different reproductive performance.
papers.abstract: Sustainable swine production hinges on optimizing sow reproductive efficiency, yet mechanisms driving healthy litter size and weak piglet rates remain unclear. This study categorized sows into high (group H) and low (group L) healthy litter size groups based on median performance. Multi-omics analyses (16S rRNA sequencing, metagenomics, and serum metabolomics) revealed distinct fecal microbiota and metabolic profiles between groups. papers.title: Uncovering the Hidden Risks: How PLA and PLGA Microplastics Disrupt Gut Microbiota and Metabolic Health.
papers.abstract: Biodegradable plastics are often promoted as an eco-sustainable alternative to conventional polymers. However, their potential to degrade into microplastics still poses significant health risks. Commonly used materials such as polylactic acid (PLA) and poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) have been widely adopted across various industries. papers.abstract: Western dietary patterns are major drivers of cardiometabolic dysfunction, partly mediated by gut microbiome dysbiosis and sustained inflammation along the gut-brain axis. In this study, we investigated whether a synbiotic formulation combining Limosilactobacillus (L.) fermentum strains with polyphenols, quercetin, and resveratrol could mitigate cardiovascular and neuroinflammatory alterations induced by a Western diet. Male Wistar rats were assigned to three groups receiving a standard diet, a

papers.title: Higher BMI is associated with vaginal microbiome alterations in women with PCOS.
papers.abstract: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is commonly associated with obesity and metabolic disturbances. Although gut and vaginal microbiome changes have been linked to PCOS, the independent role of body mass index in these alterations remains unclear. This study aimed to compare gut and vaginal microbiomes in women with PCOS and healthy controls, emphasizing the impact of body mass index. Seventy-five women were enrolled, including 55 with PCOS and 20 healthy controls. Participants were stratified into

papers.title: A genotoxin associated with colorectal cancer linked to gut dysbiosis in children with cystic fibrosis.
papers.abstract: Cystic fibrosis (CF) alters the gastrointestinal microbiome from an early age, leading to significant changes in microbial composition and functionality. This study explores the physiological and microbiological factors contributing to dysbiosis in children with cystic fibrosis (cwCF), characterized by an increase in potentially pathogenic Escherichia coli and a decrease in beneficial anaerobes, such as Bacteroides. We employed an in vitro medium representative of the nutritional environment of

papers.title: Reshaping of the fecal proteome and metaproteome in obese patients 2 years after bariatric surgery.
papers.abstract: Bariatric surgery provides effective and durable weight loss in patients with severe obesity. Both the surgical procedure and postoperative management, including dietary and behavioral changes, are known to induce long-term alterations in gut microbiota composition. However, their impact on microbial and host proteome profiles remains poorly understood. In this prospective, multicenter cohort study, we compared the fecal metaproteome profile of 45 patients with severe obesity at preoperative bas

papers.title: Cholecystectomy-associated gut microbiota dysbiosis promotes colorectal carcinogenesis: from epidemiological controversy to FXR-centered mechanistic insights.
papers.abstract: Cholecystectomy is one of the most common surgeries worldwide, yet its association with colorectal cancer (CRC) risk remains controversial. Recent advances have linked gut microbiota and bile acid alterations to this risk. This review synthesizes epidemiological evidence and molecular mechanisms, focusing on the microbiota-bile acid-FXR signaling axis, and proposes a personalized prevention framework. Epidemiological data reveal consistent patterns: proximal colon cancer predominance, higher ris
papers.journal: Journal of the Egyptian National Cancer Institute

papers.title: Dietary tryptophan supplementation prevents sepsis by enhancing macrophage bacterial defense through GPR37 activation.
papers.abstract: Sepsis, a critical and life-threatening condition, is one of the most common causes of death among inpatients. Emerging evidence suggests that active metabolites derived from gut microbe-associated metabolism of dietary essential amino acid L-tryptophan (Trp) help the host in combating infectious diseases. This study aims to investigate the mechanisms through which these active metabolites regulate sepsis progression.

papers.title: Gut microbiota-derived tryptamine activates AhR-NRF2 signaling to modulate airway epithelial barrier function in allergic asthma.
papers.abstract: Allergic asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterized by epithelial barrier dysfunction and dysregulated immune responses. Emerging evidence indicates that gut microbiota dysbiosis contributes to asthma pathogenesis through the gut-lung axis, with microbial metabolites such as tryptamine (TRP) playing critical immunomodulatory roles. However, the precise mechanisms linking gut microbiota-derived tryptophan metabolites to airway epithelial barrier integrity remain incompletely un

papers.title: Faecalibacterium-derived spermidine mediates the amelioration of fatty liver hemorrhagic syndrome by inulin in laying hens.
papers.abstract: Fatty liver hemorrhagic syndrome (FLHS) is a critical disease threatening the laying hen industry. Inulin, a widely used prebiotic, has shown promise in alleviating metabolic disorders, but its role in mitigating FLHS in laying hens is not fully understood. Here, we investigated the effects and underlying mechanisms of inulin-mediated alleviation of FLHS in a high-carbohydrate low-protein diet (HCD)-induced laying hen model. We found that inulin supplementation significantly ameliorated HCD-indu

papers.title: Cholera-toxin-induced disease generates epithelial-cell-derived L-lactate that promotes Vibrio cholerae growth in the small intestine.
papers.abstract: Cholera toxin (CT) promotes Vibrio cholerae colonization by altering gut metabolism to favor pathogen growth. Here, we show that CT-induced disease leads to the upregulation of mammalian lactate dehydrogenase A (LDHA), an enzyme that catalyzes the conversion of pyruvate to L-lactate, in small intestinal epithelial cells. In a suckling mouse model, the bacterial L-lactate dehydrogenase (LldD) confers a fitness advantage to V. cholerae but not to the ΔctxAB mutant incapable of producing CT. Finall

papers.title: Polygala oligosaccharide esters improve memory disorder by restoring gut microbiota homeostasis through the regulation of the "gut-brain" axis.
papers.abstract: Yuanzhi (Polygala tenuifolia Willd.) possesses the effects of calming the spirit, enhancing intelligence, regulating the heart-kidney connection, eliminating phlegm, and reducing swelling. It is commonly used in the treatment of insomnia and forgetfulness. Previous studies have indicated that the oligosaccharide esters (OE) derived from Yuanzhi exhibit neuroprotective and memory-enhancing activities. However, its underlying mechanisms, particularly those involving the gut-brain axis, remain uncl

papers.title: Genome-resolved and culture-based atlas of the feline gut microbiome enables host-adapted probiotic development.
papers.abstract: Domestic cats (Felis catus) depend on their gut microbiome for metabolism, immunity, and pathogen defense, yet its genomic characterization remains limited. We combined large-scale metagenomics and culturomics to define the feline gut microbiome and identify indigenous probiotic candidates. Analysis of 412 feline fecal metagenomes produced 2852 strain-resolved metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) grouped into 514 species-level genome bins, including 106 putative novel taxa. This catalog revealed

papers.title: Safety and immunostimulatory potential evaluation of Limosilactobacillus fermentum isolated from millet-based fermented food in mice.
papers.abstract: The present study assessed the safety and immunostimulatory potential of phytase-producing Limosilactobacillus fermentum MS005 at doses of 7, 9 and 11 log CFU/mouse/d in male Swiss albino mice (n = 5) during a 28-day subacute toxicity study. To further delineate immunostimulatory responses, an additional group of mice received 9 log CFU/mouse/d (n = 5). MS005 administration did not induce significant alteration in body weight, organ weight, hematobiochemical end-points, or hepatic antioxidant le
papers.journal: Brazilian journal of microbiology : [publication of the Brazilian Society for Microbiology]

papers.title: An ubiquitous antibacterial toxin from human gut bacteria engenders neonatal colonization advantage.
papers.abstract: Bacteroides fragilis is a common member of the human colonic microbiota. In children, the B. fragilis Type 6 Secretion System (T6SS) has been suggested to mediate dominance between coexisting strains. We sequenced nearly 900 clinical isolates and characterized their T6SS toxin repertoire. The T6SS effector Bte3 was identified as one of the most prevalent effectors within this human isolate cohort. Bte3 exhibited the ability to intoxicate both B. fragilis and Enterobacteriaceae in vitro, and was

papers.title: IUPHAR review. Gut Microbial Metabolites as Remote Regulators of Behavior and Neuropsychiatric Disease.
papers.abstract: The gut-brain axis has emerged as a fundamental pathway through which gut-derived microbial metabolites exert remote control over brain development, neural circuit function and behavior. This Review synthesizes evidence that key microbial metabolites including short-chain fatty acids, tryptophan derivatives, bile acids and trimethylamine N-oxide modulate neuroimmune, neuroendocrine and synaptic signaling in a context-dependent manner, influencing whether the brain maintains homeostasis or progre

papers.title: Gut microbiota-derived extracellular vesicles as autonomous regulators of host immunity and gut-organ axes: Cellular mechanisms of cross-kingdom communication in inflammatory, metabolic, and neurodegenerative diseases.
papers.abstract: Accumulating empirical evidence suggests that extracellular vesicles derived from gut microbiota (GM-EVs) function as independent biological entities that facilitate inter-kingdom communication between microorganisms and host cells. In addition to reflecting the composition of microbial communities, GM-EVs convey bioactive components including proteins, lipids, metabolites, and regulatory nucleic acids, which empower them to traverse biological barriers and modulate host immune, metabolic, and n

papers.title: Gut commensal Parabacteroides goldsteinii-derived CDCA mediates FXR signalling to improve mucosal barrier function.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome plays a crucial role in maintaining barrier integrity. However, it remains urgently needed to mining the gut microbiota that contribute to intestinal mucus barrier and to understand their biological mechanisms. In this study, the Lueyang black-boned chicken (LBC, a Chinese native breed) exhibited higher abundances of Bacteroidetes and Parabacteroides and stronger intestinal barrier function than Arbor Acres (AA, a commercial breed) broilers. Microbiota transplantation from LBC

papers.title: Metabolic engineering strategies for neuroactive postbiotic production: Current progress and perspectives.
papers.abstract: The gut-brain axis (GBA) is a bidirectional communication network regulated in part by gut microbial metabolites that influence neurological, metabolic, and behavioral functions. Among these metabolites, neuroactive postbiotics such as γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), serotonin, dopamine, and indole derivatives, have emerged as promising candidates for gut-brain modulation applications. Despite their therapeutic potential, industrial production of these compounds rema

papers.title: AS-IV attenuates nigral NLRP3 inflammasome in a Parkinson's disease mouse model via gut microbiota.
papers.abstract: Astragaloside IV (AS-IV) neuroprotection against rotenone (ROT)-induced Parkinson's pathology was examined via microbiota-gut-brain axis mechanisms. Methods included intraperitoneal AS-IV, fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) from AS-IV-treated mice, and AS-IV-modulated microbiota transplantation. Substantia nigra dopaminergic neurons/microglia were evaluated by confocal imaging, while NLRP3, caspase-1, IL-1β, and α-synuclein (α-syn) levels were quantified via Western blot. papers.title: Atractylodes lancea Polysaccharides Protect against Staphylococcus aureus-Induced Lung Injury by Remodeling Gut Microbiota and Restoring Tryptophan Metabolism.
papers.abstract: Gut microbiota and their metabolites may shape pulmonary immune responses and infection progression. Plant-derived polysaccharides, such as Atractylodes lancea polysaccharides, have shown prebiotic activity, yet their protective mechanisms in pneumonia remain unclear. Here, A. lancea polysaccharides (ALPs), a water-soluble acidic fraction mainly composed of arabinose, galacturonic acid, glucose, and galactose with a predominant molecular weight of 3.52 kDa, were evaluated in a murine model of St
papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Ethanol-Tolerant Lactobacillus rhamnosus L7 Ameliorates Alcohol-Induced Liver Injury in Mice by Inhibiting the TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB Inflammatory Signaling Pathway and Restoring Gut Microbiota Homeostasis.
papers.abstract: Alcohol-related liver disease (ALD) represents a growing global public health concern, and there is still a lack of safe, effective clinical intervention approaches to date. This study aimed to evaluate the protective effect of ethanol-tolerant Lactobacillus (L.) rhamnosus L7 on ALD in mice. In this work, L. rhamnosus L7 with high ethanol tolerance was isolated from fermented grains and pit mud. The strain was intragastrically gavaged to mice with alcoholic liver injury established via the Gao-b

papers.title: The bioactive potential of red seaweed oligosaccharides obtained using autohydrolysis: Correlation between extract composition and prebiotic activity.
papers.abstract: This study evaluated, for the first time, the prebiotic-like potential of glucooligosaccharides and galactooligosaccharides derived from five red seaweeds, produced using autohydrolysis. The non-purified oligosaccharide-rich extracts were characterized in terms of composition, glycosidic linkages, and molecular weight profiles. papers.title: Integrated Genomic, Structural, and Metabolomic Profiling of Freeze-Dried Postbiotic Supernatants Reveals Multi-Target Anticancer Activity Against Caco-2 Cells.
papers.abstract: Postbiotic metabolites derived from probiotics bacteria have gained increasing attention as multitargeted anticancer agents with improved safety and stability compared to live microorganisms. In this study, we evaluated the anticancer activity of freeze-dried cell-free supernatants (CFS) obtained from Bifidobacterium bifidum and Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus against human colorectal adenocarcinoma (Caco-2) cells and integrated functional, molecular, and omics-based analyses. papers.title: Bacteroides-derived endocannabinoid-like commendamide attenuates skeletal muscle ferroptosis in vitro: implications for Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiota regulates skeletal muscle physiology, with an increasingly recognised role in Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), the most severe X-linked myopathy. Unlike previous studies, we focussed on the genus Bacteroides and its metabolites, assessing their abundance in DMD mice and patients to clarify their potential contribution to the disease.

papers.title: Strain-Specific Lactic Acid Bacteria Fermentation of Kiwifruit Juice: From Flavor Optimization to In Vitro Gut Microbiota Modulation.
papers.abstract: The deep processing of kiwifruit remains an urgent challenge. In this work, we evaluated the effects of seven lactic acid bacteria (LAB) strains on the overall quality of the kiwifruit juice. All strains exhibited robust growth, and LAB fermentation markedly improved the flavor profile of the juice, with the M29 group best preserving the characteristic kiwi fruit aroma. papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Interlinked Microbiota-Metabolite-Epigenome Dynamics in the Gut-Heart-Ovary Axis: New Perspectives on Cardiometabolic Risk in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome.
papers.abstract: Beyond reproductive dysfunction, polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) is a complex endocrine condition that significantly raises metabolic and cardiovascular morbidity in PCOSaffected individuals. There is growing evidence that the gut microbiota plays a vital role in the pathogenesis of PCOS and the cardiometabolic problems associated with it. papers.title: The Role of Microbiome-Associated Metabolites and Their Clinical Implications in Traumatic Brain Injury: A Scoping Review.
papers.abstract: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) is a major public health challenge, with heterogeneous mechanisms and limited targeted therapies. Despite advances in neurocritical care, interventions to meaningfully alter long-term outcomes have been elusive, and treatment remains largely supportive. papers.title: Aging of the adaptive immune system affects the gut microbiome and systemic levels of vitamin B6.
papers.abstract: Age-associated dysregulation of the gut microbiota is a hallmark of aging and has been linked to multiple age-related diseases, yet upstream host factors driving these changes remain incompletely defined. Extensive bidirectional crosstalk between gut microbiota and mucosal immunity has been described. papers.title: Age and sex dependent shift in murine gut microbiome reveals pathological links to host cardiovascular and metabolic pathways.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome undergoes dynamic, sex-dependent changes across the lifespan. However, comprehensive studies examining the combined effects of age and sex are limited. This study investigated both compositional and functional alterations in the gut microbiome of young (4 months, n = 14) and aged (20 months, n = 20) C57BL/6 J mice of both sexes using 16S rRNA gene (V3-V5) sequencing. papers.title: The Prebiotic 2'-Fucosyllactose Ameliorates Colitis via an Akkermansia muciniphila-Mediated Prebiotic-Microbiota-Metabolite Axis.
papers.abstract: Recent studies indicate that 2'-fucosyllactose (2'-FL), a human milk oligosaccharide, alleviates inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) by modulating the gut microbiota, yet the underlying mechanisms and key functional bacteria involved remain unclear. This study employed fecal microbiota transplantation and an antibiotic-treated mouse model to investigate the role of 2'-FL-mediated gut microbiota in lipopolysaccharide-induced colitis, with a focus on Akkermansia muciniphila (A. muciniphila). papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Intracellular acidification by microbiota-derived valeric acid facilitates trans-kingdom ecology limiting Candida parapsilosis colonization.
papers.abstract: In hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT) patients, intestinal Candida parapsilosis expansion and translocation can cause life-threatening candidemia, yet whether commensal intestinal bacteria prevent Candida expansion remains incompletely defined. Here, we trained a machine learning model on supernatant metabolomic profiles of Lachnospiraceae to identify bacteria-derived inhibitors of fungal growth, identifying valeric and butyric acids as top hits. papers.title: Sodium butyrate supplementation improves growth performance, gut health, hepatic enzyme activities, and disease resistance against Aeromonas hydrophila in Labeo rohita.
papers.abstract: Sodium butyrate (SB) is gaining attention as a dietary supplement in aquaculture because of its potential benefits for fish health and growth. This research was conducted to assess the impacts of SB concentrations on the growth, antioxidant status, digestive enzyme activities, gut morphology, hepatic enzyme activities and survival against Aeromonas hydrophila in rohu (Labeo rohita). For this purpose, SB was supplemented at 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 and 4.0 g/kg in the basal diet (30.16% crude protein).

papers.title: Study on the Alleviating Effect of Tropical Psidium guajava Basal Postbiotics on Hyperuricemia.
papers.abstract: Hyperuricemia (HUA) is a growing global health concern with a younger onset trend. Using a high-purine diet-induced HUA mouse model, this study evaluated kidney, colon, and gut microbiota damage and investigated the effects of Psidium guajava basal postbiotics (PGP). PGP reduced xanthine oxidase activity, modulated purine metabolism, and increased beneficial gut bacteria (Akkermansia muciniphila) and short-chain fatty acids (acetate, butyrate, propionate). papers.journal: Journal of agricultural and food chemistry

papers.title: Longitudinal associations of the gut microbiome with arterial stiffness in US adults: findings from the Baltimore Longitudinal Study of Aging.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome affects arterial stiffness in experimental murine models; however, evidence in human longitudinal studies is lacking. In this study, we investigated longitudinal between-person (average) and within-person (change) associations of microbiome features with arterial stiffness. We assessed the fecal microbiome using whole genome metagenomic sequencing, and arterial stiffness using carotid-femoral pulse wave velocity (cfPWV). papers.title: Role of the gut microbiota-bile acid axis in liver fibrosis based on microbiomics and targeted metabolomics.
papers.abstract: Effective diagnoses and treatments for liver fibrosis (LF) are lacking. The gut microbiota (GM)-bile acid (BA) axis is critically associated with LF development and may represent a potential target for delaying or reversing LF.

papers.title: Gut-bone axis in rheumatoid arthritis: microbiota-driven barrier dysfunction, immune crosstalk, and therapeutic strategies.
papers.abstract: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by persistent synovial inflammation and progressive bone destruction in which immune dysregulation plays a central role. Recent evidence has highlighted the gut-bone axis as a critical framework linking gut microbiota to skeletal and immune homeostasis. Gut microbiota dysbiosis disrupts intestinal barrier integrity by altering tight junction proteins and increasing intestinal permeability, facilitating microbial translocatio

papers.title: Gut microbiota-derived Tryptophanol driven by N-Carbamylglutamate alleviates premature ovarian failure through inhibiting oxidative stress.
papers.abstract: Premature ovarian failure (POF) affects 1-5% of women under 40 years old and is characterized by granulosa cells (GCs) apoptosis and follicular atresia. However, the pathogenesis of POF is complex and lacks effective prevention and treatment strategies. N-carbamylglutamate (NCG), a bioactive substance known for its antioxidant properties; however, whether it can alleviate POF remains unclear.

papers.title: Re-arming checkpoint blockade in MSS colorectal cancer: A precision-microbiome playbook from mechanisms to clinic.
papers.abstract: Immune checkpoint blockade transforms outcomes for the 15% of colorectal cancers (CRCs) with mismatch-repair deficiency; yet most tumours remain refractory. Beneficial gut microbes can change this. Akkermansia muciniphila, Bacteroides fragilis, and short-chain fatty acid producers prime dendritic cells to produce interleukin (IL)-12, polarise Th1 cells, and reinvigorate CD8+ T-cells. papers.title: Acid-enhanced drinkable hydrogel co-delivering riboflavin-loaded nanozymes for orchestrated inflammation-immune modulation and redox homeostasis in colitis therapy.
papers.abstract: Conventional inflammatory bowel disease therapies are limited by inefficient colon-targeted delivery, systemic side effects, and a failure to rectify the underlying oxidative stress and immune dysregulation. Riboflavin (RAF) possesses ideal antioxidant and immunomodulatory properties for colitis therapy but suffers from rapid proximal absorption. papers.title: Phenotypic, Genomic, and In Vivo Characterization of a Host-Derived Limosilactobacillus fermentum RLF77 with Potential to Prevent Post-weaning Diarrhea in Rabbits.
papers.abstract: Following the 2020 ban on antibiotic growth promoters in animal feed, diarrheal disease in weaned rabbits has become an increasingly important challenge for the rabbit industry. Due to their superior intestinal adaptability and colonization potential, host-derived probiotics are considered promising alternatives. In this study, Limosilactobacillus fermentum RLF77, a lactic acid bacterium, was isolated from the intestinal contents of healthy young rabbits. Strain RLF77 showed a favorable safety p

papers.title: Substrate recognition and cleavage by mucin degrading O-glycopeptidases from the gut microbe Bacteroides caccae.
papers.abstract: O-glycopeptidases are enzymes that hydrolyze the peptide bonds in glycoproteins by a mechanism that involves specific recognition of O-linked glycans on the substrate. Bacteroides caccae, an accomplished mucin degrader, is a member of the human gut microbiota with sixteen genes encoding putative O-glycopeptidases in the peptidase_M60 family. papers.title: Bifidobacterium breve207-1 protects against later-life colitis following early-life antibiotic exposure by enhancing intestinal barrier and immune homeostasis.
papers.abstract: The early colonization of bacteria has a significant impact on immune system development. The disruption of gut microbiota by antibiotics in early life is a major risk factor for pediatric inflammatory bowel disease. This study explores how Bifidobacterium breve 207-1 modulates gut microbiota and immune responses after early antibiotic exposure, and whether such intervention mitigates DSS-induced colitis later in life.

papers.title: Bifidobacterium breve PRL2020 mitigates antibiotic-induced gut symptoms and dysbiosis in children treated with amoxicillin or amoxicillin-clavulanate.
papers.abstract: Diarrhea caused by amoxicillin (A) or amoxicillin-clavulanate (AC) is common in children, and the induced dysbiosis is often characterized by a severe reduction in bifidobacteria. However, bifidobacteria are extremely antibiotic-sensitive, and their co-administration with antibiotics is likely unwise. The B. breve PRL2020 strain is an A- and AC-resistant probiotic. papers.journal: International microbiology : the official journal of the Spanish Society for Microbiology

papers.title: Clockwork symbiosis: The vital interplay of microbiota and human chronobiology.
papers.abstract: The human microbiota and host circadian system engage in a bidirectional dialogue with consequences for metabolism, immunity, and barrier physiology. This narrative review examines the microbiota-chronobiology interface across the gut and selected extra-intestinal niches and uses the term "clockwork symbiosis" as an organizing framework for this crosstalk. papers.title: Gut microbiome-blood cholesterol crosstalk: towards personalized strategies for dyslipidemia.
papers.abstract: Hypercholesterolemia is a major risk factor for cardiovascular diseases, influenced by both genetic predisposition and multifactorial acquired factors, including diet, lifestyle, obesity, type 2 diabetes, and gut microbiota dysbiosis. papers.title: Chenodeoxycholic acid redirects the bile acid synthetic pathway to limit pro-inflammatory neutrophil infiltration and alleviate colitis.
papers.abstract: Patients with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) exhibit a dysregulated bile acid pool, characterized by increased primary and decreased secondary bile acids, largely due to gut microbiota dysfunction. However, the impact of colitis on hepatic bile acid synthesis remains poorly understood. papers.title: Uric acid promotes dietary lipid absorption through microbiome and metabolomic remodeling via a liver-gut endocrine axis.
papers.abstract: Hyperuricemia closely correlates with obesity, yet high uric acid (UA) is largely viewed as a consequence or biomarker of obesity rather than a causal driver. Integrating human clinical analyses with animal studies, we redefine UA as a liver-derived endocrine regulator that drives obesity by modulating gut ecology. papers.title: The gut-brain-gonad axis mediates salinity adaptation in an invasive turtle: causal evidence from microbiota transplantation and metabolite supplementation.
papers.abstract: Preliminary studies have shown that salinity stress can impair the differentiation of spermatogonial stem cells (SSC) in red-eared slider (Trachemys scripta elegans) through the gut-brain-gonad axis, thereby affecting their reproductive ability. However, a direct causal link between salinity-induced gut microbiota alterations and reproductive suppression remains unclear. papers.title: Microbiota-gut-brain axis and treatment resistance in epilepsy: a multicentre prospective study protocol (CARE).
papers.abstract: Approximately one-third of people with epilepsy (PWE) experience resistance to treatment, including pharmacological therapies, epilepsy surgery, vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) and dietary interventions such as the ketogenic diet (KD). Emerging evidence suggests that the gut microbiota may influence seizure susceptibility and treatment response through the microbiota-gut-brain axis, potentially contributing to treatment resistance. papers.title: Microbiota dysbiosis influences immune system and muscle pathophysiology of dystrophin deficient mice.
papers.abstract: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is a progressive, severe muscle-wasting disease caused by mutations in DMD, encoding dystrophin, that leads to loss of muscle function with cardiac/respiratory failure and premature death. papers.title: Lacto-N-biose Improves Acute Colitis in Mice by Modulating Gut Microbiota and Metabolites.
papers.abstract: Lacto-N-biose (LNB) as the core structure among human milk oligosaccharides remained insufficiently characterized in terms of the potential to ameliorate colitis. papers.title: Sodium butyrate induces cuproptosis by regulating the HDAC1-SLC31A1 axis in hepatocellular carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the primary type of liver malignancy, which stands as the third leading contributor to cancer-related death worldwide. Sodium butyrate (NaB), a gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acid with known anticancer activity, remains poorly understood in HCC. papers.title: Gut-brain axis crosstalk in patients with Alzheimer's disease and high body mass index: Gut dysbiosis, blood-brain barrier disruption, and neuroinflammation.

papers.title: The gut-brain axis in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases: a systematic review of microbiota-derived biomarkers and novel therapeutic approaches.
papers.abstract: The altered gut microbiota substantially impacts the onset and progression of Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Parkinson's disease (PD), the two most widely studied neurodegenerative conditions. Microbiome-derived metabolites have been increasingly associated with disease onset, progression, and therapeutic targets in neurodegenerative disorders. papers.title: Ameliorative effects and mechanisms of Inonotus hispidus on PCOS via regulation of gut microbiota-ovarian metabolism axis and ferroptosis inhibition.
papers.abstract: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a prevalent endocrine metabolic disorder with limited therapeutic options. papers.abstract: Although peripheral-brain crosstalk regulates energy metabolism, its role in depression remains unclear. papers.abstract: Cholesterol biosynthesis and bile acid metabolism are tightly controlled by the Farnesoid X receptor (FXR), which integrates bile acid signaling with lipid, glucose, and inflammatory pathways to maintain metabolic homeostasis. papers.abstract: A series of novel benzothiazole-indole acetamides (9a-n) was designed and synthesized as potential anticancer agents, and their antiproliferative activities were evaluated against three cancer cell lines: A549 (human lung cancer), SW480 (human colon cancer), and HepG2 (human liver cancer). papers.title: Ferulic acid ameliorates metabolic dysfunction associated with fatty liver disease by regulating macrophage polarization through spermidine derived from gut microbiota.
papers.abstract: This study aimed to investigate the efficacy and mechanism of Ferulic acid (FA) in a high-fat diet (HFD)-induced murine model of Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), focusing on the gut microbiota-polyamine-macrophage polarization axis.

papers.title: Pediococcus acidilactici PA53 Improves Emotional Well-Being and Gut Health while Modulating Microbiota Composition in Older Adults: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial.
papers.abstract: The gut-brain axis links intestinal microbiota with emotional health, a critical concern in aging populations experiencing gut dysbiosis.

papers.title: Adjuvant Lactiplantibacillus plantarum Lp05 modulates gut microbiota and alleviates symptoms during Helicobacter pylori eradication.
papers.abstract: Helicobacter pylori eradication therapy is often accompanied by gastrointestinal adverse effects and gut microbiota dysbiosis. The role of specific probiotic strains in mitigating these issues requires further clinical evidence. In a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, 72 H. pylori-positive patients received bismuth quadruple therapy alongside either Lactiplantibacillus plantarum Lp05 or a placebo for 2 weeks, followed by a 4-week extension of probiotic or placebo alone. papers.title: Fecal microbiota transplantation from Hezuo pigs alleviates intestinal inflammatory injury in Clostridium perfringens type C-infected piglets via modulation gut microbiota and intestinal barrier.
papers.abstract: Clostridium perfringens type C (CpC)-induced piglet diarrhea is a major challenge in the swine industry. This study aimed to investigate the protective effects of fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) from Hezuo pigs against intestinal injury in Duroc×Landrace×Yorkshire (DLY) suckling piglets infected with CpC. papers.title: High-resolution metabolomic analysis of stool reveals expanded biomarkers of C. difficile colitis and insights into pathophysiology.
papers.abstract: Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) is facilitated by gut microbiome disruption and associated metabolic disturbances. While many prior studies have focused on the microbial composition of CDI stool, fewer have explored the fecal metabolome using untargeted high-resolution metabolomics (HRM). To characterize the metabolic phenotype of CDI, we performed untargeted HRM on stool samples from 55 CDI patients and 72 healthy controls. papers.title: Gut Microbiota and Autoimmune Diseases: The Therapeutic Potential of Probiotics.
papers.abstract: In recent years, increasing attention has been directed toward the role of gut microbiota in the pathogenesis of autoimmune diseases such as type 1 diabetes (T1D), rheumatoid arthritis (RA), and multiple sclerosis (MS). Numerous studies have shown that gut dysbiosis-an imbalance in the intestinal microbial community-can influence host immune responses by modulating inflammatory pathways, gut barrier integrity, and immune cell function. papers.title: Linkage of gut microbiota dysbiosis to chronic kidney disease in patients with graded proteinuria levels.
papers.abstract: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is highly prevalent worldwide, with proteinuria serving as a key diagnostic and prognostic indicator. The concept of the "gut-kidney axis" suggests that gut microbiota dysbiosis may contribute to CKD pathogenesis; however, its relationship with proteinuria severity remains unclear. papers.title: Aspartame promotes intestinal tumor progression via Akkermansia muciniphila -mediated 5-hydroxytryptophan production.
papers.abstract: Aspartame, one of the most widely consumed artificial sweeteners globally, has been associated with increased cancer risk in epidemiological studies, yet the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. This study investigated the effects of subchronic aspartame exposure on intestinal tumorigenesis using the Apcmin/+ spontaneous intestinal tumor mouse model and elucidated the mechanistic pathways involved. papers.title: Akkermansia muciniphila-derived L-norleucine modulates FABP1-dependent fatty acid transport.
papers.abstract: Fatty acids undergo re-esterification to form triglycerides or are directly oxidized for energy production following absorption. Fatty acid binding protein 1 (FABP1), a key transporter highly expressed in both hepatic and intestinal tissues, directs the metabolic fate of absorbed fatty acids. papers.title: Integrated multi-omics analysis reveals gut dysbiosis and altered energy metabolism in Chinese ALS patients.
papers.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a fatal neurodegenerative disorder with a complex etiology. Emerging evidence implicates gut microbiota dysbiosis in ALS pathology via the gut-brain axis, yet the specific integrative profile of the gut microbiome, virome, and metabolome, particularly in Chinese patients, remains incompletely characterized. papers.title: Lanzhou Lily (Lilium davidii var. unicolor) Extract Alleviates Chronic Stress-Induced Mood Disturbances by Suppressing Neuroinflammation and Modulating the Gut-Brain Axis in Mice.
papers.abstract: Chronic stress is a major contributor to mood disturbances through dysregulation of the gut-brain axis and neuroinflammatory responses. Lilium davidii var. unicolor (Lanzhou lily), a traditional medicinal and edible plant, contains bioactive compounds with anti-inflammatory potential. This study investigated the effects of Lanzhou lily extract (LLE) on chronic restraint stress (CRS)-induced behavioral and physiological changes in mice. papers.abstract: Host-microbiota interactions play a critical role in modulating the serotonin (5-HT) system, which is essential for maintaining intestinal homeostasis. Akkermansia muciniphila and Faecalibacterium prausnitzii are well-recognized commensal bacteria with anti-inflammatory properties and barrier-enhancing functions. papers.title: Oligosaccharide prebiotics in functional foods and therapeutics: innovations and challenges.
papers.abstract: Oligosaccharide prebiotics, such as inulin, fructooligosaccharides (FOS), and galactooligosaccharides (GOS), have demonstrated significant effects on gut microbiota and host health across in vitro, animal, and clinical studies. papers.title: Deconfounded, quantitative microbiome profiling identifies robust multiple sclerosis markers and clinical covariate associations.
papers.abstract: Despite a wealth of gut microbiota studies in multiple sclerosis (MS), consistent results are lacking. Here, we study confounder effects and use of quantitative microbiome profiling (QMP) in 228 MS patients (103 untreated) and 2860 population controls (Flemish Gut Flora Project (FGFP)). papers.title: Sulfide dynamics at the gut-microbiota interface: diet, oxygen and redox interplay.
papers.abstract: Teeming with microbes, the unique biogeography of the gut is shaped by interactions between diet, host and microbial metabolism. Hydrogen sulfide represents one such plane of interaction in the lower gut where it is largely the product of microbial activity. Sulfide oxidation by host epithelial cells helps shape a severely hypoxic luminal environment in which obligate anaerobes thrive and furnish among other products, butyrate, a fuel of choice for colonocytes. papers.title: Zip14 (Slc39a14) metal transporter dysfunction leads to Akkermansia dysbiosis and inflammation in intestinal epithelium via epigenetic changes.
papers.abstract: Zinc is recognized as an important component of immune function in animals, operating through many different mechanisms. Metal transporter Zip14 (Slc39a14) is up-regulated during pro-inflammatory conditions and increases zinc uptake into cells, including enterocytes. As reported here, global Zip14 deletion in mice leads to excessive intestinal mucus accumulation and markedly increased fecal levels of the enteric microbe Akkermansia muciniphila. papers.title: From gut microbiota metabolism to microvascular injury: Exploring the role and mechanisms of gut microbiota in obesity-induced coronary microcirculation dysfunction.
papers.abstract: Obesity, a global epidemic, drives coronary microvascular dysfunction (CMD), a precursor to cardiovascular disease, via gut microbiota dysbiosis. Clinical evidence shows a 47.7% reduction in coronary flow reserve among obese patients, highlighting obesity's pivotal role in CMD pathogenesis. papers.title: Microbiota‑derived indole‑3‑propionic acid reprograms bone marrow stem cell fate via PPARγ suppression to rescue osteoporosis.
papers.abstract: Osteoporosis (OP) is increasingly recognised as a disorder driven by impaired lineage allocation of bone marrow stromal cells (BMSCs), characterised by a shift from osteogenesis toward adipogenesis under conditions such as oestrogen deficiency and oxidative stress. papers.abstract: This study investigated how alcohol exacerbated chronic prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome (CP/CPPS) and assessed gut microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies.

papers.abstract: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic, disabling and incurable autoimmune disease. It has been widely recognized that gut microbial dysbiosis is an important contributor to the pathogenesis of RA, although distinct alterations in microbiota have been associated with this disease. papers.abstract: Bifidobacteria dominate the gut microbiota of breast-fed infants, and are strongly associated with human health, including immune regulation, colonization resistance, and protection against inflammation. However, bifidobacteria persist at high abundance after weaning in only a subset of individuals, and the factors that regulate intestinal persistence of bifidobacteria are poorly understood.
